lessons.title: FESS Anatomy Orientation
lessons.summary: Review key endoscopic landmarks and safe surgical planes before intervention.

questions.prompt: What are the two critical landmarks you must identify before starting uncinectomy?
questions.hint: Think of landmarks that orient you to orbit and skull base safety.
questions.modelAnswer: The lamina papyracea and skull base orientation are key safety landmarks. Confirming these landmarks helps prevent orbital injury and CSF-related complications.
questions.explanation: In endoscopic sinus surgery, early orientation around lamina papyracea and skull base avoids dissection into dangerous planes.

questions.prompt: How would you stratify a CRS patient before deciding on surgery?
questions.hint: Include symptom burden, endoscopic findings, CT extent, and response to maximal medical therapy.
questions.modelAnswer: I would assess symptom severity and quality-of-life impact, confirm disease on nasal endoscopy and CT, and ensure adequate trial of maximal medical therapy before surgical planning.
questions.explanation: Surgery is usually reserved for medically refractory disease with objective evidence and persistent burden.

questions.prompt: Name one intraoperative strategy that lowers complication risk during FESS.
questions.hint: Think about orientation and controlled stepwise dissection.
questions.modelAnswer: A stepwise dissection with frequent anatomic re-orientation and maintaining dissection close to known landmarks reduces complication risk.
questions.explanation: Complications often happen after loss of orientation; deliberate verification at each step improves safety.

lessons.title: Complication Prevention Checklist
lessons.summary: High-yield actions to prevent orbital and skull base injuries.

questions.prompt: What red-flag endoscopic sign should make you pause and reassess orientation?
questions.hint: Unexpected bleeding or tissue plane mismatch can be a warning.
questions.modelAnswer: Unexpected tissue resistance, atypical bleeding, or loss of expected landmark relationships should trigger immediate pause and reorientation before proceeding.
questions.explanation: Recognizing disorientation early prevents escalation into major complications.

questions.prompt: How would you respond to suspected orbital fat exposure intraoperatively?
questions.modelAnswer: Stop dissection immediately, avoid further manipulation, reassess anatomy and hemostasis, and escalate based on severity with multidisciplinary input if required.
questions.explanation: Early recognition and minimal further trauma are key to limiting morbidity.

questions.prompt: When should an inexperienced trainee convert to supervised takeover?
questions.hint: Use predefined safety thresholds, not confidence alone.
questions.modelAnswer: If orientation is uncertain, bleeding obscures planes, or anatomy deviates from expected landmarks, immediate supervised takeover should occur.
questions.explanation: Standardized escalation criteria protect patient safety and improve training outcomes.

lessons.title: Epistaxis Triage and Stabilization
lessons.summary: Build an emergency-first approach for active nasal bleeding.

questions.prompt: What are your first three priorities in severe epistaxis?
questions.modelAnswer: Priorities are airway protection, hemodynamic assessment/stabilization, and initial bleeding control with direct compression and topical vasoconstrictor support.
questions.explanation: Systematic ABC management prevents delayed recognition of instability.

questions.prompt: Which clues suggest posterior instead of anterior epistaxis?
questions.hint: Think bilateral bleeding and difficult source visualization.
questions.modelAnswer: Persistent heavy bleeding, blood into oropharynx, bilateral flow, and poor visualization of anterior source suggest posterior epistaxis.
questions.explanation: Posterior bleeds are often harder to control and may require escalation.

questions.prompt: When should you involve interventional radiology or surgery?
questions.hint: Consider uncontrolled bleeding despite standard escalation.
questions.modelAnswer: If bleeding persists despite appropriate packing and resuscitation, or recurrent severe episodes occur, escalation to surgical/IR control is indicated.
questions.explanation: Timely escalation reduces transfusion burden and instability.

lessons.title: Bedside Vertigo Exam and Red Flags
lessons.summary: Use practical bedside tests to triage dizzy patients safely.

questions.prompt: What historical feature best distinguishes vertigo from non-vestibular dizziness?
questions.hint: Focus on sensation quality and movement relation.
questions.modelAnswer: A rotational or motion illusion, often worsened by head movement, strongly supports vestibular vertigo over nonspecific lightheadedness.
questions.explanation: Characterizing symptom quality narrows diagnosis early.

questions.prompt: When is HINTS exam appropriate and what is one danger in misuse?
questions.hint: Use only in continuous acute vestibular syndrome by trained examiners.
questions.modelAnswer: HINTS is appropriate in continuous acute vestibular syndrome with spontaneous nystagmus, and misuse in intermittent or non-AVS patients can cause dangerous false reassurance.
questions.explanation: Clinical context determines HINTS utility and safety.

questions.prompt: Give one central red flag requiring urgent escalation.
questions.modelAnswer: Any focal neurological deficit (e.g., limb weakness, dysarthria, severe gait ataxia) is a central red flag and requires urgent stroke-oriented escalation.
questions.explanation: Central pathology must be prioritized when neurological signs appear.
